text: During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes an incredibly dilated gastric remnant. Adhesions involving the BP limb are identified and divided using energy, including a thick adhesion at the proximal portion. The third portion of the duodenum shows some improvement. Adhesions involving the rullum are also divided. A G-tube is then placed into the gastric remnant.

The surgical team prepares for diagnostic laparoscopy and gastroduodenoscopy via the remnant G-tube. Ports are placed in their previous positions, and adhesions are lysed. The three limbs of the gastric bypass are visible. The third portion of the duodenum appears distended. The transverse mesocolon is divided to expose the duodenum, revealing the underlying vena cava.

The surgical team completes the dissection to expose the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels. A pediatric endoscope is advanced through the gastrostomy into the duodenum, navigating through a narrowed area at the third portion of the duodenum where pulsations from the SMA are visible. The team confirms the diagnosis of SMA syndrome and proceeds with a duodenal jejunal anastomosis using the common channel jejunum, placing an anchoring suture.

The surgeon creates enterotomies and performs a stapled anastomosis, approximating the common enterotomy with interrupted sutures. A stapled closure is completed, and an anti-obstruction stitch is placed. A drain is left in the area of the anastomosis. Postoperative imaging shows no dilatation of the gastric remnant and contrast flow through the anastomosis.
